blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا ٱلشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (١)
When the sun is wound up, (1)
وَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ ٱنكَدَرَتْ (٢)
when the stars scatter, (2)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ سُيِّرَتْ (٣)
when the mountains are set moving, (3)
وَإِذَا ٱلْعِشَارُ عُطِّلَتْ (٤)
when the pregnant camels are neglected, (4)
وَإِذَا ٱلْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ (٥)
when the wild beasts are mustered, (5)
وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ سُجِّرَتْ (٦)
when the seas are set afire, (6)
وَإِذَا ٱلنُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (٧)
when the souls are assorted, (7)
وَإِذَا ٱلْمَوْءُۥدَةُ سُئِلَتْ (٨)
when the girl buried-alive will be asked (8)
بِأَىِّ ذَنۢبٍ قُتِلَتْ (٩)
for what sin she was killed. (9)
وَإِذَا ٱلصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ (١٠)
When the records [of deeds] are unfolded, (10)
وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ كُشِطَتْ (١١)
when the sky is stripped off, (11)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجَحِيمُ سُعِّرَتْ (١٢)
when hell is set ablaze, (12)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُزْلِفَتْ (١٣)
when paradise is brought near, (13)
عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّآ أَحْضَرَتْ (١٤)
then a soul shall know what it has readied [for itself]. (14)

(Commentary)

The Surah reminds of the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) by mentioning some of its portents (أشراطها) and what occurs therein. It describes it as a day in which what man has performed of deeds shall be disclosed to him. Then, it describes the Quran as being among that which was delivered to the Prophet, may Allah bless him and his progeny and grant them peace (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), by a heavenly messenger—namely, the Angel of Revelation (ملك الوحي)—and that it is not a satanic casting, nor is the Prophet, may Allah bless him and his progeny and grant them peace (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), possessed (مجنون) or touched by Satan.

It appears that the Surah is among the early (العتائق) Surahs revealed at the beginning of the Prophetic mission (البعثة), as evidenced by its exoneration of him, may Allah bless him and his progeny and grant them peace (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), from the charge of madness (الجنون) with which they accused him. They had accused him of this in the early stages of the call (الدعوة), and Surah "Nun" (ن), which is also among the early Surahs, similarly contains his exoneration from it.

The Surah is Meccan without dispute.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ "When the sun is wound up," (81:1). Winding up (التكوير) is to wrap something in a circular fashion, like the wrapping of a turban (العمامة) around the head. Perhaps what is meant by the winding up of the sun is the darkening of its mass in an encompassing manner, used metaphorically.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ "when the stars are scattered," (81:2). The swooping (انكدار) of a bird from the air is its pouncing toward the earth. Accordingly, what is meant is the falling of the stars, as suggested by His saying: وَإِذَا الْكَواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ "when the planets are scattered," (82:2). It is also possible that it comes from inkidar (انكدار) in the sense of changing and accepting murkiness (الكدورة), in which case it would mean the fading of their light.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْجِبَالُ سُيِّرَتْ "when the mountains are set moving," (81:3). This refers to the setting in motion that afflicts them due to the earthquake of the Hour (زلزلة الساعة), such that they are crushed, becoming scattered dust (هباء منبثا) and turning into a mirage (سرابا), according to what the Glorified has mentioned in various places of His speech.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْعِشَارُ عُطِّلَتْ "when the pregnant camels are neglected," (81:4). It is said that al-ʿishār (العشار) is the plural of ʿusharāʾ (عشراء), like nifās (نفاس) is the plural of nufasāʾ (نفساء). It refers to a pregnant she-camel that has completed ten months of pregnancy; she is called ʿusharāʾ until she gives birth, and sometimes she is called so even after giving birth. These were among the most precious forms of wealth (نفائس المال) for the Arabs.

The neglect (تعطيل) of the ʿishār means leaving them abandoned with no shepherd to tend them and no guardian to protect them. The sentence contains an indication by way of metonymy (الكناية) that the precious wealth for which man competes will remain on that day with no owner to possess or manage it, because they are preoccupied with themselves away from everything else, as He said: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ "That day every man among them will have a concern to keep him occupied." (80:37).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ "when the wild beasts are mustered," (81:5). Al-wuhūsh (الوحوش) is the plural of wahsh (وحش), which refers to animals that do not associate with humans, such as beasts of prey and others.

The apparent meaning of the verse, given its occurrence in the context of verses describing the Day of Resurrection, is that the wild beasts are gathered (محشورة) just like humans. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ "There is no animal on land, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves. We have not omitted anything from the Book. Then they will be mustered toward their Lord." (6:38).

As for the details of their state after the gathering (الحشر) and what their ultimate end will be, nothing has been mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, nor in the reliable narrations (الأخبار) that would clarify this. However, it may be inferred from His saying in the verse of al-Anʿām: أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ "communities like yourselves" and His saying: ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "We have not omitted anything from the Book" some points by which the situation is clarified in general, which would not be hidden from the critical and contemplative thinker. It has also been said that the gathering of the wild beasts is among the portents of the Hour (أشراط الساعة) and not what occurs on the Day of Resurrection itself, meaning their emergence from their forests and dens.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ "when the seas are set afire," (81:6). Al-tasjīr (التسجير) has been interpreted as kindling fire (إضرام النار) and also as filling (الملإ). According to the first meaning, it means: "when the seas are kindled into fire," and according to the second: "when the seas are filled."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ "when the souls are paired," (81:7). As for the souls of the felicitous (السعداء), they are paired with the women of Paradise; He, the Exalted, said: لَهُمْ فِيها أَزْواجٌ مُطَهَّرَةٌ "there will be chaste mates for them therein," (4:57), and He said: وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ "and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris." (44:54). As for the souls of the wretched (الأشقياء), they are paired with the companions from the devils (قرناء الشياطين); He, the Exalted, said: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْواجَهُمْ وَما كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ "[The angels will be told:] ‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates and what they used to worship’" (37:22), and He said: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهو لَهُ قَرِينٌ "We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent." (43:36).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْمَوْؤُدَةُ سُئِلَتْ بِأَيِّ ذَنْبٍ قُتِلَتْ "when the girl-child buried alive is asked for what sin she was killed," (81:8-9). Al-mawʾūdah (الموءودة) is the girl who is buried alive. The Arabs used to bury girls alive for fear of the shame (العار) that might befall them because of them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُمْ بِالْأُنْثى ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ يَتَوارى مِنَ الْقَوْمِ مِنْ سُوءِ ما بُشِّرَ بِهِ أَيُمْسِكُهُ عَلى هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُ فِي التُّرابِ "When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face turns black and he chokes with suppressed agony. He hides from the people out of the distress of what he has been given news of: shall he keep it in humiliation, or bury it in the ground?" (16:58-59).

In reality, the one to be questioned about the killing of the buried girl is her father who buried her, so that justice may be served and vengeance taken from him. However, the one mentioned as being questioned in the verse is the buried girl herself; she is asked about the reason for her killing as a kind of insinuation (التعريض) and rebuke (التوبيخ) toward her killer, and as a prelude for her to ask Allah for justice against her killer, until he is questioned about her killing and she is vindicated against him. Thus, the speech is similar to His saying, the Exalted, regarding Jesus (peace be upon him): وَإِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’" (5:116).

It has also been said that attributing the questioning to the buried girl is a form of mental trope (المجاز العقلي), and what is meant is that she is the one about whom the question is asked, similar to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْعَهْدَ كانَ مَسْؤُلاً "Indeed the faithful fulfillment of covenants will be questioned." (17:34).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ "when the records are unrolled," (81:10). That is, for the reckoning (الحساب); and the records (الصحف) are the books of deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا السَّماءُ كُشِطَتْ "when the sky is stripped off," (81:11). In al-Majmaʿ, it is stated: al-kashṭ (الكشط) is to uproot something from a state of intense adhesion. Thus, it corresponds to its being folded, as in His saying: وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ "and the heavens will be folded up in His right hand," (39:67), and His saying: وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ بِالْغَمامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلائِكَةُ تَنْزِيلاً "The day when the sky will be rent asunder with clouds and the angels will be sent down in a grand descent." (25:25), and other verses that express this meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْجَحِيمُ سُعِّرَتْ "when hell is set ablaze," (81:12). Al-tasʿīr (التسعير) is the stirring of fire until it flares up.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْجَنَّةُ أُزْلِفَتْ "and when paradise is brought near," (81:13). Al-izlāf (الإزلاف) is bringing close, and what is meant is bringing it near to its people for entry.

His saying, the Exalted: عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما أَحْضَرَتْ "then every soul shall know what it has readied [for itself]," (81:14). This is the apodosis (جواب) of "when" (إذَا). What is meant by "soul" (نفس) is the genus, and what is meant by "what it has readied" is the deed it performed. It is said: "I readied the thing" (أحضرت الشيء), meaning I found it present (حاضرا), just as it is said: "I praised him" (أحمدته), meaning I found him praiseworthy (محمودا).

Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30).

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (When the sun is wound up), he said: "It becomes black and dark." Regarding وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ (when the stars are scattered), he said: "Their light fades." Regarding وَإِذَا الْجِبالُ سُيِّرَتْ (when the mountains are set moving), he said: "They move as He said: تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ (You see the mountains, which you suppose to be stationary, while they drift like passing clouds)." Regarding His saying: وَإِذَا الْعِشارُ عُطِّلَتْ (when the pregnant camels are neglected), he said: "The camels are neglected when the creation dies, so there will be no one to milk them." Regarding His saying: وَإِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ (when the seas are set afire), he said: "The seas that surround the entire world will turn into fires." Regarding وَإِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (when the souls are paired), he said: "With the black-eyed houris (الحور العين)."

In the same source, in a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَإِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (when the souls are paired), he said: "As for the people of Paradise, they are paired with the goodly and beautiful ones (الخيرات الحسان). As for the people of the Fire, with every person among them is a devil"—meaning the souls of the disbelievers and hypocrites are joined with the devils—"so they are their companions."

I say: It appears that the phrase "meaning etc." is from the words of the narrator.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and al-Daylami narrated from Abu Maryam that the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his progeny and grant them peace) said regarding His saying: إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ (When the sun is wound up): "It is wound up in Hell." Regarding وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ (when the stars are scattered), he said: "They are scattered in Hell. And everyone who was worshipped besides Allah shall be in Hell—except for Jesus son of Mary and his mother—and had they been pleased to be worshipped, they would have entered it."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ (when the records are unrolled), he said: "The records of deeds." Regarding His saying: وَإِذَا السَّماءُ كُشِطَتْ (when the sky is stripped off), he said: "It is nullified."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Nu'man ibn Bashir who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his progeny and grant them peace) saying: وَإِذَا النُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ (when the souls are paired): "They are the two men who perform the [same] deed; they enter Paradise and the Fire [together]."

Ayahs

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلْخُنَّسِ (١٥)
So I swear by the stars that return, (15)
ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْكُنَّسِ (١٦)
the comets, (16)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا عَسْعَسَ (١٧)
by the night as it approaches, (17)
وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَا تَنَفَّسَ (١٨)
by the dawn as it breathes: (18)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (١٩)
it is indeed the speech of a noble apostle, (19)
ذِى قُوَّةٍ عِندَ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ (٢٠)
powerful and eminent with the Lord of the Throne, (20)
مُّطَاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ (٢١)
one who is obeyed and is trustworthy as well. (21)
وَمَا صَاحِبُكُم بِمَجْنُونٍ (٢٢)
Your companion is not crazy: (22)
وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢٣)
certainly he saw him on the manifest horizon, (23)
وَمَا هُوَ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ (٢٤)
and he is not miserly concerning the Unseen. (24)
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ (٢٥)
And it is not the speech of an outcast Satan. (25)
فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ (٢٦)
So where are you going? (26)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٧)
It is just a reminder for all the nations, (27)
لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ (٢٨)
for those of you who wish to walk straight; (28)
وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٩)
but you will not wish unless it is wished by Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (29)

(Commentary)

This is a vindication of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the charge of madness (الجنون) — with which they had accused him — and a defense of what he brings — the Quran — from the interference of Satan (الشيطان). It affirms that it is the speech of Allah, the Exalted, delivered to him by the Angel of Revelation who is never treacherous in his mission, and that it is a reminder (ذكر) for all the worlds, a guide by Allah’s permission for those among them who seek guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالْخُنَّسِ الْجَوارِ الْكُنَّسِ — I swear by the stars that recede, the ones that run their courses and hide. (81:15-16). Al-Khunnas (الخنس) is the plural of khānis (خانس), similar to how tullab is the plural of tālib. Al-Khunūs (الخنوس) means contraction, lagging behind, and concealment. Al-Jawārī (الجواري) is the plural of jāriyah (جارية); al-jary (الجري) refers to rapid movement, a metaphor derived from the flowing of water. Al-Kunnas (الكنس) is the plural of kānis (كانس); al-kunūs (الكنوس) refers to a wild animal, such as a gazelle or a bird, entering its kunās (كناسة), which is the home it has taken for itself and its place of rest.

The fact that His saying, "I swear by the stars that recede..." is followed by His saying, "And by the night as it retreats, and by the dawn as it breathes," supports the view that what is meant by "the stars that recede, the ones that run their courses and hide" are the stars in their entirety, or some of them. However, the characteristics of the movement of some stars are more fitting and clearly applicable to the mentioned attributes sworn by: receding (الخنوس), running (الجري), and hiding (الكنوس). These are the five "wandering" planets (السيارات الخمس المتحيرة): Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury. In their observed movements, they exhibit direct motion (استقامة), retrograde motion (رجعة), and stationarity (إقامة). They travel and run with a uniform motion for a time — which is direct motion; they contract, lag behind, and recede for a time — which is retrograde motion; and they pause from movement, both direct and retrograde, for a time — as if they are wild animals hiding in their dens — which is stationarity.

It has also been said that they refer to all stars; their "receding" is their disappearance during the day under the light of the sun, their "running" is their observed travel at night, and their "hiding" is their setting in the west and their concealment.

It has further been said that they refer to wild bovine or gazelles. It is not improbable that the mention of wild bovine or gazelles is by way of example, and the intended meaning is wild animals in general.

In any case, the closest of these views is the first, the second is remote, and the third is even more remote.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا عَسْعَسَ — by the night as it retreats, (81:17). This is conjoined to "the stars that recede," and "as it retreats" (إِذا عَسْعَسَ) is a qualifier for the night. The term al-’as’asah (العسعسة) is applied both to the onset of night and its departure. Al-Raghib said: "وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا عَسْعَسَ means it approached and departed, and that occurs at the beginning of the night and its end. Thus, al-’as’asah and al-’asās refer to the thinness of darkness, which occurs at the two ends of the night." (End of quote). It is more appropriate, given the connection of the sentence to His saying, "and by the dawn as it breathes," that it refers to the departure of the night.

It has been said that it refers to the onset of the night, but this is remote for the reason already mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالصُّبْحِ إِذا تَنَفَّسَ — and by the dawn as it breathes, (81:18). This is conjoined to "the stars that recede," and "as it breathes" (إِذا تَنَفَّسَ) is a qualifier for the dawn. The dawn is described as "breathing" because of the spreading of its light over the horizon and its pushing away of the darkness that had enveloped it. This is a type of metaphor (استعارة) by likening the dawn — having risen after the darkness had shrouded the horizons — to someone who was surrounded by the exhaustion of arduous tasks and then found a gap of time in which to rest and breathe. Thus, its illumination of the horizon is considered its breathing; this is what is understood from some commentators.

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned another aspect, saying in al-Kashshaf: "If you ask: What is the meaning of the dawn breathing? I say: When the dawn approaches, it brings with it a spirit and a breeze, so that was metaphorically made its breath." (End of quote). The previous view is closer to the mind.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ — it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger, powerful and established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy. (81:19-21). This is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The pronoun in "it is indeed" (إِنَّهُ) refers to the Quran, or to the preceding verses of the Surah insofar as they are Quran, evidenced by His saying, "the speech of a noble messenger." The "messenger" (الرسول) intended here is Gabriel (جبريل), as the Exalted said: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart by Allah’s will (2:97).

The attribution of the "speech" (القول) to him in his capacity as a messenger indicates that the speech belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and its attribution to Gabriel is the attribution of the message to the messenger. Allah has described him with six attributes by which He praised him.

His saying, "messenger" (رَسُولٍ) indicates his mission and his delivery of the revelation of the Quran to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). His saying, "noble" (كَرِيمٍ) means possessing nobility and honor with Allah through His honoring him. His saying, "powerful" (ذِي قُوَّةٍ) means possessing immense power and strength. His saying, "established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne" (عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ) means possessing a high status with Allah; al-makānah (المكانة) is proximity and rank. His saying, "obeyed there" (مُطاعٍ ثَمَ) means obeyed in the presence of Allah; for there are angels whom he commands and they obey him. From this, it appears that he has assistants among the angels whom he commands and they follow his orders. His saying, "trustworthy" (أَمِينٍ) means he does not betray what he is commanded; he conveys the revelation and message he was entrusted with without any alteration.

It has been said that the "messenger" to whom these attributes apply is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but this is as you see — it does not harmonize with the subsequent verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما صاحِبُكُمْ بِمَجْنُونٍ — and your companion is not crazy. (81:22). This is conjoined to His saying, "it is indeed the speech..." and is a rebuttal of their accusation of madness (الجنون) against him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The expression "your companion" (صاحِبُكُمْ) regarding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a refutation of their charge of madness and a vindication of his presence — as has been said. It contains an allusion that he is your companion who has lived among you, socializing with you throughout his life, and you know him best. You have found him to be of perfect intellect, gravity of judgment, and truthfulness of speech. One who possesses such attributes is not to be accused of madness.

The description of Gabriel with the aforementioned attributes of praise, without the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), does not indicate his superiority over the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is because the discourse is aimed at explaining that the Quran is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, sent down to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from Him by way of revelation, not from the delusions of madness or the promptings of a devil. What serves this purpose is the explanation of the integrity of the path of descent and the glorification of the one who brings it down — the active participle (المنزل) — by mentioning his noble attributes and emphasizing his freedom from error and betrayal. As for the one upon whom it is sent down, the purpose only relates to him insofar as it points toward repelling the doubts regarding his condition. This was achieved by negating the madness they accused him of and referring to him as "your companion," as previously clarified. This is what has been said.

However, within the folds of the speech of the Exalted are noble attributes of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) such that there remains no doubt regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) superiority over all the angels. Indeed, Allah made all the angels prostrate to the human being, who is His vicegerent (خليفة) on earth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ — Certainly he saw him on the manifest horizon. (81:23). The subject pronoun in "he saw him" (رَآهُ) refers to the "companion," and the object pronoun refers to the noble messenger, who is Gabriel.

The "manifest horizon" (الأفق المبين) is the clear side. It appears to be what He referred to in His saying: وَهُوَ بِالْأُفُقِ الْأَعْلى — while he was on the highest horizon. (53:7).

The meaning is: I swear that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw Gabriel while Gabriel was in the manifest horizon, which is the highest horizon (الأفق الأعلى) among all horizons, as befits the world of the angels.

It has been said the meaning is: He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw Gabriel in his original form where the sun rises, which is the highest horizon from the direction of the east.

The problem with this is that there is no evidence in the wording for it, especially regarding the vision being of his original form; for seeing him in any form he manifests is still seeing him. It seems this view was taken from what was mentioned in some narrations that he saw him at the beginning of the mission while he was between the heaven and the earth, sitting on a chair; this is understood as a manifestation (التمثل).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما هُوَ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ — He is not stingy with the Unseen, (81:24). The pronoun refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). What is meant by "the Unseen" (الغيب) is the revelation (الوحي) descending upon him. Al-Danīn (الضنين) is an intensive adjective from al-dann (الضن), meaning stinginess (البخل). It means that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is not stingy with anything revealed to him; he does not conceal it, withhold it, or change it by substituting any part of it or the whole with something else. Rather, he teaches the people as Allah taught him and conveys to them what he was commanded to convey.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — nor is it the speech of an outcast devil. (81:25). This is a negation of the Quran being attributed to the promptings of a devil (شيطان) in a sense broader than the path of madness. For "devil" (الشيطان) means an evildoer, and "the outcast devil" (الشيطان الرجيم), just as it is applied in the speech of the Exalted to Iblis and his progeny, is also applied to the evildoers among the rest of the Jinn. The Exalted said: قالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْها فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ — He said, ‘Then get out of it, for you are indeed an outcast. (38:77), and He said: وَحَفِظْناها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — and We have guarded them from every outcast devil. (15:17).

The meaning is that the Quran is not from the embellishments of Iblis and his hosts, nor from the promptings of the evildoers among the Jinn as they cast them upon the madmen.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ — So where are you going? (81:26). The Glorified has clarified in the seven preceding verses what the truth is regarding the matter of the Quran, repelling their doubts about it concerning the madness and other things they accused the one who brought it with, despite the brevity of the verses. He explained, first, that it is the speech of Allah, and the reliance of this reality is upon the verses of challenge (آيات التحدي). Second, that its descent was through the mission of a celestial angel of noble stature and great status, who is the trustworthy one of revelation, Gabriel; there is no barrier between him and Allah, nor between him and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and there is no diverter from himself or others that turns him away from receiving it, preserving it, or conveying it. Third, that the one upon whom it was sent down and who recites it to you — who is your companion whose condition is not hidden from you — is not mad as they slander him. He has seen the angel carrying the revelation and received from him, and he is not a concealer of what is revealed to him nor a changer of it. Fourth, that it is not from the embellishments of Iblis and his hosts, nor from the promptings of some of the evildoers among the Jinn.

The result of this clarification is that the Quran is a book of guidance (هدى) by which whoever desires to remain steadfast upon the truth is guided, and that is His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ — It is just a reminder for all the nations, (81:27) etc.

Thus, His saying, "So where are you going?" is a preamble and preparation for mentioning the result of the previous clarification, which is a declaration of their straying in what they perceive regarding the matter of the Noble Quran — that it is from the occurrences of madness or the false embellishments of Satan.

The interrogation in the verse is for rebuke (توبيخي), and the meaning is: If the matter is as such, then where are you going, leaving the truth behind you?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ — It is just a reminder for all the nations, (81:27). That is, a reminder for the groups of people, whoever they may be, through which they can gain insight into the truth. Some discussion on the like of this verse has already passed.

His saying, the Exalted: لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَسْتَقِيمَ — for those of you who wish to walk straight. (81:28). This is an appositive (بدل) for His saying, "for all the nations," aimed at explaining that the actualization of benefiting from this reminder is conditional upon their wishing to remain steadfast (الاستقامة) upon the truth, which is to be characterized by firmness in servitude and obedience.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ — But you do not wish unless it is wished by Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (81:29). The discussion on its meaning has passed in the likes of this verse.

According to what the context provides, the verse is in the sense of repelling a potential objection (دفع الدخل). For it is possible they might imagine from His saying, "for those of you who wish to walk straight," that they possess independence in the volition (مشية) for steadfastness; if they wish, they remain steadfast, and if they do not wish, they do not, such that Allah would have a need for them in the steadfastness He desires from them.

So this was repelled by the fact that their volition is dependent upon the volition of Allah, the Glorified. They do not wish for steadfastness unless Allah wishes that they wish for it. Thus, the voluntary acts of the human being are willed by Allah through the path of his own will; that is, Allah wills that the human being perform such-and-such an act out of his own will.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: Sa’id ibn Mansur, al-Faryabi, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated through various chains from ‘Ali regarding His saying: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالْخُنَّسِ — "He said: They are the stars that hide by night and recede by day, so they are not seen."

In Tafsir al-Qummi regarding His saying: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالْخُنَّسِ — "He said: That is, I swear by the Khunnas, which is a name for the stars. الْجَوارِ الْكُنَّسِ — He said: The stars that hide by day so they do not appear."

In al-Majma’: "بِالْخُنَّسِ — and they are the stars that recede by day and appear by night. And (الْجَوارِ) is an attribute for them because they run in their orbits. الْكُنَّسِ is also among their attributes — because they hide (تكنس), meaning they conceal themselves in their constellations — just as gazelles conceal themselves in their dens. They are five stars: Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury, narrated from ‘Ali. وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا عَسْعَسَ — meaning when it departs with its darkness, narrated from ‘Ali."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: "وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا عَسْعَسَ — He said: When it becomes dark. And (الصُّبْحِ إِذا تَنَفَّسَ) — He said: When it rises."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Mu’awiyah ibn Qurrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Gabriel: 'How beautifully your Lord has praised you: powerful and established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy — so what was your power? And what was your trustworthiness?'

He said: 'As for my power, I was sent to the cities of Lot — and they were four cities, and in each city were four hundred thousand fighting men besides the offspring — and I carried them from the lower earth — until the inhabitants of heaven heard the sounds of the chickens and the barking of the dogs — then I plummeted with them and killed them. As for my trustworthiness, I was never commanded with anything such that I bypassed it to something else.'"

I say: The narration is not free from issues, and they have weakened Ibn ‘Asakir, especially in what he alone narrates.

In al-Khisāl, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever says seventy times every day in Sha’ban: 'I seek forgiveness from Allah, besides whom there is no god but He, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful — the Living, the Sustainer, and I turn to Him in repentance,' it shall be written in the manifest horizon (الأفق المبين)." He said: "I asked: 'And what is the manifest horizon?' He replied: 'A plain before the Throne in which rivers flow — and in it are vessels numbering the stars.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in a hadith attributed to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Regarding His saying: وَما هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — He said: It means the soothsayers (الكهنة) who were among the Quraysh — so He attributed their speech to the speech of the devils who were with them — speaking through their tongues — so He said: وَما هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — like those."
